title: Fever without source in infants
executiveSummary: Assess age, overall appearance, and red flags. Stratify the risk of serious bacterial infection and decide on outpatient management, observation, or admission.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Record the infant’s exact age and confirm that the fever is real (rectal or axillary temperature according to local protocol).
2. Obtain a complete set of vital signs: HR, RR, BP, temperature, and SpO₂.
3. Prioritize assessment of the infant’s overall appearance (alertness, tone, interaction, feeding) over the absolute temperature value.
4. Screen for red flags: lethargy, poor perfusion, petechiae/purpura, retractions, refusal to feed, or a bulging fontanelle.
5. Assess hydration and perfusion; document immunization status and relevant past medical history.
6. Stratify risk by age group and agree on the management pathway with the medical team (outpatient workup, observation, or admission).
7. Obtain age- and risk-appropriate samples as indicated: blood culture prior to antibiotics when applicable, urine culture (bag or sterile method depending on age), and additional studies per criteria.
8. If observing, monitor and re-assess overall appearance every 1–2 hours.
9. Educate the family about warning signs and criteria for urgent re-consultation.
10. Refer or admit immediately if any red flag is present at any point during assessment.

## Scientific rationale

### Objective

Standardize the initial evaluation of infants presenting with fever without an apparent source.

### Age-based stratification (general approach)

| Age | General management |
| --- | --- |
| 0–28 days | Consider admission and a complete workup (meningitis, UTI, bacteremia) |
| 29–60 days | Laboratory evaluation and urine culture; blood culture according to protocol and clinical appearance |
| 61–90 days | Use validated criteria (Rochester, Philadelphia, or local guidance) |
| > 3 months | Assess overall appearance; seek clinical sources; outpatient management if well-appearing |

### Nursing assessment (high-yield)

- Complete vital signs (HR, RR, BP, temperature, SpO₂).
- Hydration and perfusion status.
- Fontanelle, petechial rash, retractions, and feeding refusal.
- Vaccination and relevant history documentation.

### Red flags

- Lethargy, hypotonia, or inconsolable irritability.
- Poor perfusion, hypotension, or disproportionate tachycardia.
- Petechiae or purpura.
- Retractions, grunting, cyanosis, or apnea.
- Persistent vomiting or complete refusal of feeds.

> **Source:** American Academy of Pediatrics. Clinical Practice Guideline: Febrile Infants 8 to 60 Days Old. Pediatrics. 2021.

### Samples (per medical indication)

- Blood culture prior to antibiotics when applicable.
- Urine culture (bag collection vs sterile method depending on age and local protocol).
- Additional tests based on age, risk stratification, and clinical findings.

bibliography.citation: American Academy of Pediatrics. Clinical Practice Guideline: Febrile Infants 8 to 60 Days Old. Pediatrics. 2021.
SATI — Área Pediatría. Recursos y lineamientos en cuidados críticos pediátricos.
Sociedad Latinoamericana de Cuidados Intensivos Pediátricos (SLACIP) — Consenso LATAM manejo de sepsis en niños.
Weiss SL, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign Pediatric Guidelines 2026 — reconocimiento precoz en lactante febril.
